It only needs to handle timeouts if it's using multiple processes. The worker will periodically check for new work while waiting for jobs to finish. If you're running with only one process, the timeout doesn't mean anything. Get and put will always return immediately. It's correct to throw out the timeout argument, but it would also be reasonable not to indiscriminately ignore any keyword argument and just match the signatures of the multiprocessing.Queue functions:

    put(obj[, block[, timeout]])
    get([block[, timeout]])

Just adding optional block and timeout arguments that you ignore looks more intentional and makes it more clear that you're not throwing out important arguments. If someone were to call DequeQueue().put(obj=x), your code would throw out the obj argument and result in an error.

I'm okay with dropping the Server header if none is set by the WSGI app, but we should absolutely set a `Via` header if a `Server` header is set by the WSGI app (likely means that waitress is being used as some sort of proxy).

Mostly because it feels like code smell that we're having to explicitly export a particular standard module into our GPI namespace so that another part of code can continue to work. `bootstrap.py` should not need to know about `JobTime`. What if some third-party plugin requires some other module to be present, would we add them all here? When you're working on a coding problem and you're finding that you're having to add strange hacks to make things work, that's very often a sign that there's something wrong. This is coupling unrelated parts of the software together, reducing encapsulation and destroying locality when reading the code. Ganga is built on these hacks and I've been working hard to make things maintainable in the future, the only way Ganga will survive with the amount of manpower we have is if it becomes simpler and smaller. Constantly patching problems on a half-a-million LOC project does not scale.

I don't know if Thread specifies this, but my first thought would be to use other aproach. Here if let's say packet is destined to ff05::1 which is registered by one of our SED, we would add two IPv6 packets to the queue:

1) One without MPL header, destined to ff05::1
2) One with MPL header and IP-in-IP header, destined to FF03::FC

Personally, i would prefer to not modify this part of ip6.cpp, and allow to create IP-in-IP packet. However, i would modify mesh-forwarder part related to forwarding multicast message to SEDs. And instead, check IPv6 address included in the inner IPv6 header, if the outer IPv6 header destination address is set to FF03::FC.

SEDs must be subscribed to FF03::FC address anyway:

> To forward multicast packets that have scope larger than realm-local, all Thread devices receive and process MPL Data Messages sent to the realm-local ALL_MPL_FORWARDERS address.

I guess if this approach doesn't violate specification, it would remove a need for changes in the ip6.cpp file which simplifies the overall code.
